title: NESSi 2.0: The Non-Equilibrium Systems Simulation package version 2.0
authors: Fabian Künzel, Michael Schüler, Denis Golež, Yuta Murakami, Sujay Ray, Christopher Stahl, Jiajun Li, Hugo U. R. Strand, Philipp Werner, Martin Eckstein
abstract: Nonequilibrium Green's functions provide a powerful framework for studying quantum many-body dynamics including the laser-induced dynamics in solids. The Non-Equilibrium Systems Simulation package (NESSi) offers an efficient platform for such simulations, ranging from perturbative approaches like nonequilibrium $GW$ to nonequilibrium dynamical mean-field theory. However, simulations based on nonequilibrium Green's functions become computationally demanding when the dynamics span a large temporal range, such as from sub-femtosecond electron dynamics to the picosecond dynamics of collective modes. Due to the memory integral in the Kadanoff-Baym equations, which serve as equations of motion for nonequilibrium Green's functions, the computational cost scales as $\mathcal{O}(N_t^3)$ with the number of timesteps $N_t$, and the memory requirement scales as $\mathcal{O}(N_t^2)$. In this work, we extend NESSi by incorporating techniques that aim to overcome this bottleneck: (i) By truncating the memory integrals in the KBE to a maximum of $N_c$ timesteps, the computational complexity is reduced to $\mathcal{O}(N_tN_c^2)$, and the memory requirement to $\mathcal{O}(N_c^2)$. Provided that the results converge with respect to the cutoff $N_c$, memory truncation allows to extend the simulations to significantly longer times. (ii) We introduce functionalities to describe nonequilibrium steady states, i.e. time-translationally invariant nonequilibrium states. Such states are relevant for transport settings, and they provide an approximate description of slowly evolving (prethermal) nonequilibrium states.
summary: NESSi 2.0 is an updated software package for simulating non-equilibrium quantum many-body systems. It introduces memory-truncation techniques to enable much longer time-scale simulations and new tools for studying non-equilibrium steady states. This allows for more efficient study of phenomena like photo-induced phase transitions and thermalization.
main_problem: The computational cost of solving Kadanoff-Baym equations for non-equilibrium many-body systems scales prohibitively as O(Nt^3) in CPU time and O(Nt^2) in memory, limiting simulations to short time scales.
main_result: The NESSi 2.0 package introduces memory truncation to reduce complexity to O(NtNc^2) and implements new functionalities for simulating time-translationally invariant non-equilibrium steady states (NESS).
method: The package uses Keldysh formalism and Non-Equilibrium Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT), implementing a moving Green's function window for memory truncation and Fourier-space solvers for steady states.
model_or_system: The software is designed for strongly correlated electron systems, including the Hubbard model, Mott insulators, and SYK models, specifically focusing on laser-induced dynamics and interaction quenches.
key_observables: Retarded, lesser, and advanced Green's functions, self-energy, spectral functions, and density matrices.
important_parameters: Memory cutoff (Nc), time step (h), total real-time (t_max), and inverse temperature (beta).
main_assumptions: The memory truncation assumes the self-energy decays to zero after a certain time cutoff, and steady-state approximations assume the system is quasi-stationary or weakly coupled to reservoirs.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Green's function data structures; Figure 2 illustrates the memory-truncated domain and moving window; Figure 9 compares NESS and real-time results; Figure 10 shows numerical convergence with respect to frequency grid and timestep.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the NESSi 2.0 update, details the mathematical framework for memory truncation and steady-state simulations, describes the new C++/Python data structures, and provides benchmarks comparing NESS and real-time propagation.
why_interesting: It provides a significant algorithmic advancement for simulating long-time many-body dynamics and steady states, which is crucial for studying thermalization and relaxation in driven quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Green’s function domain and data structures on a discrete time grid. The Hermitian domain (9) for a two-time Green’s function G(t, t′) is shown by the filled squares (the Matsubara component is not shown here). The class herm_matrix stores the values of a Green’s function on the Hermitian domain up to a maximum timestep Nt, which is sufficient to represent a Hermitian Green’s function with the symmetry G = G‡. Filled squares with dots indicate the time slice n. The data on one time slice n of a Green’s function can be stored using the class herm_matrix_timestep. The timestep on the real axis is denoted by h and hτ is the timestep on the Matsubara axis.

Figure 2: Domains for data structures on a discrete time grid. The memory truncated domain, defined by Eqs. (19) and (20), is indicated by dark squares, for a cutoff nc = 2. The domain of a memory truncated time slice T [G]nc n (Eq. (22)) is shown by the squares with a red dot. Squares with a solid boundary denote the triangular window ∆[G]nc n , while squares with a dashed boundary show the extension of the triangular window (23) to the moving window M[G]nc n [Eq. (24)].
Figure 3: The functions |Σ<(t, t′)| (a) and |G<(t, t′)| (b) in the initial time window 0 ≤t, t′ ≤32.

Figure 4: (a) The functions |ΣR,<(t, t′)| and |GR,<(t, t′)| as a function of the time difference t−t′ at a given time slice (t = 32) on a logarithmic scale. (b) The function |GR k (t, t′)| as a function of the time difference t −t′ at a given time slice (t = 32) on a logarithmic scale, for different values of ϵk, where ϵk = 0 corresponds to the Fermi edge. Note the different vertical scale in the two plots.

Figure 5: Momentum occupation ρk(t) for selected values of ϵk, obtained with the memory-truncated time evolution with different tc. Different line colors represent different values of ϵk, close to the Fermi energy (ϵk = 0), in the middle of the band (ϵk = 1), and close the band edge (ϵk = 2). Different line-styles distinguish different values of tc.
